name: Jo Jones
abstract: Jonathan David Samuel Jones (October 7, 1911 – September 3, 1985) was an American jazz drummer. A band leader and pioneer in jazz percussion, Jones  anchored the Count Basie Orchestra rhythm section from 1934 to 1948.

quotes.text: At the Savoy Ballroom. It happened the same night that Mr. Benny Goodman was playing at Carnegie Hall, doing his jazz concert, because Zutty, Gene Krupa, came up at the battle. Meanwhile I went down with the Basie rhythm section to play with Benny at Carnegie Hall, but Walter Page, Freddie, and Basie played in the Benny band. I did not. And Buck played. It's on the records that they finally re-issued. This is January 1937. But after the concert I went on back up to the place, but after the concert we got in a taxi and come up there, we still continued and Chick Webb was playing and Basie's band was playing. It was a very memorial night. Matter of fact it was a memorial day because at the Criterion Theatre, Mr. Duke Ellington woke up New York at 10 o'clock at the Criterion Theatre - he opened up with the East St. Louis 'Toodle-Oo' and New York did not go to sleep until 7 or 8 o'clock in the morning. It started on a Sunday morning and on a Monday they did not go to sleep. Everybody just stayed awake. I never will forget that the that Martin Black because there was a time in my life I had the thing going
quotes.caption: Recalling the night of Benny Goodman's 1937 Carnegie Hall concert

quotes.text: He was a dancer first. You know he did Carnegie Hall; he was a dancer.
quotes.caption: Jo Jones recalls a dancer performing at Carnegie Hall

about_quotes.speaker: Chris Parker
about_quotes.evidence: I would read the whole thing beginning to end and then read about the session if they had session details and then look at the credits. Oh my God Tony Williams on drums, Jo Jones on drums or whoever the drummer was.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chris Parker interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, January 5, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Mickey Roker
about_quotes.evidence: I fell in love with Kenny Clarke and Papa Jo Jones... The guy that I listened to in my developing stages... You listen to Papa Jo Jones. These are the guys that I like, that I listen to
about_quotes.doc_title: Mickey Roker interviewed by Michael Woods, Majesty of the Seas, May 31, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: my two main teachers were Big Sid Catlett and Jo Jones. ... Big Sid and Jo really called me aside and said “look, here’s how you play the brushes...
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 12, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Hentoff Nat
about_quotes.evidence: He had what he called his kiddies: young musicians that he mentored. For some reason I was very privileged. He made me one of his kiddies.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hentoff Nat — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Snooky Young and Gerald Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: And the rhythm section was of course Freddie Green, Jo Jones, Eddie Jones and Sonny Payne and Count Basie.
about_quotes.doc_title: Snooky Young and Gerald Wilson interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Taylor
about_quotes.evidence: And from that point on, Jo was one of my mentors. So I mean he had a profound influence on my career.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Taylor interviewed by Michael Woods, New York City, New York, July 27, 1995
